pattern: When a shared item can substitute for two separate requirements at once, the only quantities of it ever worth buying are none, exactly enough to cover the smaller requirement, or exactly enough to cover the larger one -- everything in between is dominated.
hints: If you never bought any combined item, the cost is just buying singles for both requirements separately. Ask yourself: when could swapping some singles for combined items ever help?

Buying a combined item is only worthwhile relative to buying two singles when its cost beats the sum of what it replaces, or when 'wasting' its extra contribution on an already-satisfied requirement is still cheaper than paying for a single item you'd otherwise need. That means the number of combined items worth considering is a small, fixed set of quantities, not a full search.

Compare exactly three strategies and take the cheapest: buy zero combined items (singles cover everything); buy combined items up to the smaller of the two requirements (then top up the remainder of the larger one with singles); and buy combined items up to the LARGER of the two requirements (potentially overshooting the smaller one entirely, useful when the combined item is cheap enough that overshoot beats buying any singles of the other type).

edge_inputs.description: one requirement is zero, only the other needs single purchases
combined item cheap enough that overshooting the smaller requirement still wins
combined item costs exactly the sum of both singles, a tie that should not overshoot
combined item slightly cheaper than the sum of singles by one unit
large but oracle-tractable requirements, costBoth at minimum cost so overshoot dominates
large but oracle-tractable requirements, costBoth at maximum cost so singles dominate

stress.description: random small-to-moderate costs and needs, matched against a brute force that tries every quantity of combined items up to need1+need2
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    cap = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    cost1 = rng.randint(1, 1000000)
    cost2 = rng.randint(1, 1000000)
    costBoth = rng.randint(1, 1000000)
    need1 = rng.randint(0, cap)
    need2 = rng.randint(0, cap)
    return [cost1, cost2, costBoth, need1, need2]
stress.note: needs capped so the brute-force oracle (O(need1+need2)) stays fast
